title: Replacing storage heaters: what to consider first
subtitle: Comparing modern storage heaters, infrared panels, air source heat pumps, and gas boilers as replacements
excerpt: Old storage heaters run on Economy 7 overnight cheap rate at 7-12p/kWh but release heat poorly, wasting much of it before the coldest part of the morning. Modern options range from upgraded smart storage heaters (still on E7) to air source heat pumps (best long-term efficiency) or infrared panels (quick to install, lower upfront cost).
sourceNotes: Energy Saving Trust storage heater upgrade guidance. Which? guide to storage heaters vs alternatives. Electrification of Heat Demonstration Project (2022, Energy Systems Catapult) for heat pump performance in older UK homes. BEIS Boiler Upgrade Scheme criteria for Annex H2 heat pump eligibility. Economy 7 tariff structure from major UK energy suppliers.

glossaryTerms.definition: A device that uses electricity to move heat from outside air or the ground into the building, delivering 2.5-4 kWh of heat for every 1 kWh of electricity used.
Coefficient of Performance: heat delivered divided by electricity used. A COP of 3 means 3 kWh heat for 1 kWh electricity.

content.text: Storage heaters charge overnight on the Economy 7 cheap rate (typically 7-12p/kWh at 2025/26) and release heat through the day. Old units have poor heat retention and poor charge control, releasing heat through the night before the room needs it and being cold by late afternoon. There are four realistic replacements, each with a different cost, complexity, and running-cost profile.

Modern Quantum or Dimplex storage heaters have better core insulation, motorised output dampers, and demand-based charge control that forecasts the next day's heating need from weather data. Cost: a modest cost installed. Running costs are similar to an old heater (still electric, still Economy 7 rate) but the heat release is better controlled. Best choice for a property without gas and with reasonable Economy 7 access, where the occupant is not ready to change the whole system.

Infrared panels are direct electric heaters: 1 kW in = 1 kW heat out, no storage. They heat objects and bodies directly rather than the air, so the room feels warmer at a lower air temperature. Running cost is the current single-rate electricity tariff (27p/kWh, 2025/26). They are most economical in rooms used intermittently (a home office used 3 hours per day), where their fast response and simple on/off control waste little. In a house occupied all day, they cost more to run than Economy 7 storage heaters because they run at the day rate.
Option 3: air source heat pump with radiators
delivers 3-4 kWh of heat per kWh of electricity (a

of 3-4) compared to 1.0 for any direct electric heater. At 27p/kWh electricity, the effective heat cost is 7-9p/kWh: comparable to the Economy 7 cheap rate. The Boiler Upgrade Scheme (BUS) offers a modest cost toward an ASHP install in England and Wales (2026 figure). Total cost after grant: typically a modest cost depending on house size and radiator upgrades needed. Requires installing radiators or underfloor heating and pipework: a large project unsuitable for a quick swap from storage heaters.
Option 4: connecting to gas (where a supply is available)

A gas boiler connection requires a gas main nearby and a new gas supply pipe to the property (typically a modest cost). Running cost: gas at 6p/kWh, boiler efficiency around 90%, gives an effective heat cost of 6.7p/kWh. Properties off the gas network cannot use this option. Where gas is available and a heat pump is not yet viable, a gas boiler costs less to run than direct electric heating. But it sets up a gas dependency for another 15-20 year boiler life.
Good insulation (EPC C or better), long-term ownership, and gas supply: an air source heat pump with the BUS grant is the best long-term choice.
Rented home, poor insulation, or short-term plans: Economy 7 storage heaters are the best quick fix.
Rooms used only sometimes (home office, spare room): infrared panels on a timer work well. They heat the room when it is in use and switch off when it is empty.
